coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous carbonization and sulfonation of microcrystalline cellulose to obtain solid acid catalyst and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Souza, M.C.G.
Batista, A.C.F.
Cuevas, R.F.
da Silva Filho, W.J.F.
Balanta, M.A.G.
Champi, A.
de Assunção, R.M.N.
coredata.dc:description: Lignocellulosic materials have been successfully used in the production of solid acid catalysts (SACs), generally prepared in two steps: Biomass thermal or hydrothermal carbonization followed by sulfonation. The process' supernatant is usually discarded; however, this liquid fraction may contain potentially important substances. In this work, the production of SACs was carried out by simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation of Microcrystalline Cellulose (MCC) by direct reaction with concentrated sulfuric acid. The obtained catalyst have excellent characteristics, with an ionic exchange capacity of 1.3 mmol g−1. The SAC's catalytic activity was evaluated in oleic acid esterification with methanol in a molar ratio of 1:20, yielding up to 80 % conversion in 6 h of reaction at 65 °C. The supernatant showed the presence of carbon dots (CD) characterized by solution UV/Vis spectra pattern and Fluorimetry assays. Thus, the presented process allows obtaining SACs and CDs that can act as catalysts in a series of physicochemical processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfonated heterogeneous catalysts
Biomass
Microcrystalline Cellulose
Esterification
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of catalytic activity of SAC
Characterization of the liquid fraction (supernatant) to search for substances with potential for applications in energy and raw materials production
Investigation of the liquid fraction of the carbonization/sulfonation process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MCC carbonization/sulfonation steps: (a) MCC/H2SO4 reaction mixture after heating, (b) Precipitated catalyst after reaction, and (c) modified MCC after rinsing and filtration.
(a) FTIR spectrum SAC 125/1 and SAC 150/1and (b)SEM-EDS images of SACs: (a) SAC 125/1 and (b) SAC 150/1.

Characterization of the solid carbon-based acid catalyst (SAC): (a) SEM micrographs of SACs produced at 125 °C, H2SO4:MCC ratio 20:1 (v:w) for 1, 2, 4 and 6 h, (b) XRD patterns of SAC 125/2 and SAC 125/4, (c) Raman spectra of SAC125 produced with 1 h, 2 h, 4 h and 6 h of reaction and (d) TGA/DTG curves of SAC 125/1.
Evaluation of catalytic activity of SAC: Oleic Acid Esterification, Reusability of SAC after 3 reaction cycle and catalyst regeneration.
UV–Vis spectra for the liquid fractions CD 125/1 and CD 125/6, recovered from the first filtration (pH = 0) and last filtration (pH = 5).

(a) Photoluminescence spectra obtained from different wavelengths of excitation for the solution CD 125/1 at (i) pH = 0 and (ii) pH = 5 (b) PL intensity and wavelength of maximum emission peak as a function of excitation wavelength for the CD 125/1 solutions at (i) pH = 0 and (ii) pH = 5.
Synthesis of the heterogeneous acid catalyst, separation of the solid acid and the liquid extract that may present potentially important substances.
Carbon-based catalyst yield (%) and Ion Exchange Capacity (mmol g−1).
Mean and standard deviation for carbonization/sulfonation yields performed at 125 °C, H2SO4:MCC ratio 20:1 (v:w) for 1, 2, 4 and 6 h.
Mean and standard deviation for esterification yields obtained between multiple reactions using the SACs produced at 125 °C.
HPLC assays for investigation of the supernatant for fermentable sugars, furfurals, acetic and formic acids.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous carbonization and sulfonation of microcrystalline cellulose to obtain solid acid catalyst and carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Exacts and Natural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignocellulosic materials have been successfully used in the production of solid acid catalysts (SACs), generally prepared in two steps: Biomass thermal or hydrothermal carbonization followed by sulfonation. The process' supernatant is usually discarded; however, this liquid fraction may contain potentially important substances. In this work, the production of SACs was carried out by simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation of Microcrystalline Cellulose (MCC) by direct reaction with concentrated sulfuric acid. The obtained catalyst have excellent characteristics, with an ionic exchange capacity of 1.3 mmol g−1. The SAC's catalytic activity was evaluated in oleic acid esterification with methanol in a molar ratio of 1:20, yielding up to 80 % conversion in 6 h of reaction at 65 °C. The supernatant showed the presence of carbon dots (CD) characterized by solution UV/Vis spectra pattern and Fluorimetry assays. Thus, the presented process allows obtaining SACs and CDs that can act as catalysts in a series of physicochemical processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulosic materials are abundant sources of feedstock for the production of several chemicals and materials. The great challenge in the use of these sources is the development of adequate and effective methods for converting raw materials into value-added products through the biorefinery principle. The main methods involve the biomass pre-treatment to allow the separation of the lignocellulosic components, which depending on the application, it is desirable as it allows the use of cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignins, separately in the production of a series of materials and products. This principle is used in the pulp and paper industry as well as in the production of cellulose-based polymers.

In the production of solid carbon-based acid catalysts, the use of alternative lignocellulosic sources has been the subject of several works in the most recent literature, having as main parameters the type of biomass and the type of pre/treatment, among other relevant aspects. More recently, in addition to the production of solid catalysts, special attention has been drawn to the supernatant of these processes, especially to the separation and characterization of Carbon Dots. The two most applied carbonization methodologies to obtain carbonaceous material from natural sources are pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment. The partial carbonization of saccharides or lignocellulosic compounds gives rise to a rigid carbon material, consisting of aromatic polycyclic layers of carbon in a three-dimensional arrangement, containing functional groups –OH and –COOH. Sulfonation of these materials generates a stable solid with a high density of acidic sites (Hara et al., 2005; Zailan et al., 2021).

Hara et al. (2005), proposed the production of a solid acid catalyst (SAC) through the pyrolysis and sulfonation of sucrose and d-glucose, resulting in a material with good performance and reusability. Since then, several works have been published dealing with obtaining catalysts from these and other refined carbohydrates, such as starch and cellulose, and also from complex lignocellulosic materials from biomass (Abdullah et al., 2017; Hara, 2010; Lou et al., 2008).

The works cited above have in common the fact that no systematic investigation was carried out on the liquid fractions resulting from the synthesis process despite the fact that this investigation may result in the isolation of important substances that could be used in other processes of technological importance. Considering these aspects, this work presents an investigation on the use of microcrystalline cellulose as a source to produce a SAC prepared in a single step by simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation of the cellulosic material, as well as the obtaining of value-added substances in the liquid fraction generated in the process. The choice of cellulose aims to reduce the complexity of the biomass compared to compound lignocellulosic sources and to favor the possible identification and characterization of the species present in the supernatant.

Cellulose is an abundant natural biopolymer that can be used as a platform for the preparation of several substances and materials. Most of the works in the literature addresses their use in the production of solid acid catalysts and in the production of carbon dots separately, in this sense, the intention of this research is to find pathways to make the most complete possible use of the product and byproducts to produce the catalyst. In this context, the present work aims to explore the production of multiple products (solid acid catalysts and carbon dots) from the derivatization of microcrystalline cellulose by a simple one-pot synthesis method, the materials produced will be characterized to assess the suitability of the proposed method in the production of new inputs.

For the production of SAC, commercial microcrystalline cellulose (Sigma-Aldrich®, 100 % of cellulose) and concentrated sulfuric acid 98 % (Êxodo®) were used, as for the catalyst regeneration. The quantification of SAC acid sites was carried using solutions of NaCl (Vetec®) and NaOH (Dinâmica®). Oleic acid (Dinâmica®) and methanol (Vetec®) were used in the esterification reactions, while heptane (Êxodo®) and methyl heptadecanoate (Sigma-Aldrich®) were used in methyl ester quantification in GC-FID analyses. Ethanol and acetone supplied by Dinâmica® were used in the recovery and washing of spent catalyst.

The SAC were synthesized using the method of simultaneous carbonization and sulfonation, carried by the direct reaction of microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) with concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4). Carbonization/sulfonation reactions were realized at temperatures of 100, 125 and 150 °C, for periods of 1, 2, 4 and 6 h, with H2SO4:MCC ratios of 10:1 and 20:1 (v:w). The mass ratio of cellulose:concentrated sulfuric acid were 1:18.4 (w/w), and 1:36.8 (w/w), which corresponds to 5.15 % and 2.65 % of cellulose respectively (Mendaros et al., 2020; Malins et al., 2016).

For this procedure, a portion of MCC were slowly added over the adequate amount of H2SO4 under magnetic stirring in an Erlenmeyer flask to produce a homogeneous suspension, which was then placed in a thermostatic oil bath adjusted in the desired temperature. Once the reaction time was completed, the mixture was poured into cold water to halt the reaction and to precipitate the product. The material was vacuum filtered, thoroughly washed with ultrapure water to remove all acid residue and dried at 100 °C for 12 h. The supernatant was reserved for the evaluation of the presence of fermentable sugars and carbon quantum dots. Scheme 1 summarizes the experimental procedure for catalyst production.

The dried product was grinded, packed in paper cartridges and washed with hot water in a soxhlet extractor for the removal of any acid residues and weakly bounded sulfonic groups. The washing was carried over 10 cycles of hot water in the soxhlet apparel, following the drying of the material at 100 °C for 6 h.

The total acid density of the SAC was determined by ion exchange and titration, where 0,1 g of catalyst was suspended in 40 mL of NaCl 2 mol L−1 solution kept under stirring overnight. The solution was filtered by paper and separated in aliquots of 10 mL, each one titrated with NaOH 0,01 mol L−1 solution using phenolphthalein as indicator. The SAC acidity was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Acidity =
B
V B
V Total
V aliq
/
m sample
where, [B] and VB are the titrant base concentration and consumed volume, respectively; VTotal and Valiq are the total volume of the NaCl reagent solution and the volume of the titrated aliquot; msample is the mass of sample analyzed.

Additionally, the synthesized SACs were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy with attenuated total reflectance (FTIR-ATR) in an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer, for identification of the functional groups attached in the catalyst structure. Structural analyses of the materials were performed in a WITec Alpha300 R Raman Spectrometer using a 8 mW green laser at 532 nm for acquisition of the Raman spectra; and a XRD-6000 Shimadzu diffractometer for acquisition of the X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of the catalyst powder, applying Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 30 mA, with 2θ scanning interval of 5–40°. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) were performed in the interval of 25–950 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under a N2 atmosphere with flow rate of 50 mL min−1, using a TA Instruments thermogravimetric analyzer model TGA 55.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of catalytic activity of SAC
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of the SACs produced were evaluated in the esterification of oleic acid (OA) with methanol using a 20:1 M ratio of methanol:OA, 5 % of catalyst load and 6 h reaction time at 65 °C. The reaction products were separated by filtration, the recovered catalyst washed with ethanol and acetone, oven dried at 80 °C for 6 h.

The reaction products were analyzed, and the methyl esters quantified by gas chromatography with flame ionization detector (GC-FID) using Thermo® chromatographer model Focus CG, equipped with a Carbowax capillary column of poliethileneglicol and dimensions of 30mx0,32mmx0,25 μm. Analysis program: oven temperature 190 °C; sample volume: 1 μL; split ratio 1:10; sampler temperature: 250 °C; detector temperature: 250 °C; analysis time: 5 min.

The spent catalysts recovered after the first esterification experiments were reused for two more consecutive reaction cycles, being separated, properly washed and dried after each cycle; the reaction products were analyzed for quantification of methyl esters. After the third esterification cycle, the spent catalyst underwent a regeneration process, carried by mixing the spent catalyst with concentrated sulfuric acid under stirring for 1 h, following its filtration and washing with abundant water until neutralization, 20 mL of acetone and dried at 80 °C for 6 h. The regenerated catalysts were used in another esterification reaction for assessing its catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the liquid fraction (supernatant) to search for substances with potential for applications in energy and raw materials production
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatant of SAC production was characterized by chromatographic and spectroscopic techniques for the analysis of reaction byproducts in a Liquid Chromatograph with detection by diode array and refractive index in series (HPLC/DAD/R) equipped with an Aminex® HPX-87H 300 × 7.8 mm Bio-Rad column at 55 °C. A solution of H2SO4 5 mmol L−1 was used as mobile phase with a flow rate of 0.6 mL min−1. For detection and quantification were used the refractive index at 35 °C and two wavelengths in the diode array, 210 and 274 nm.

The compounds monitored were Furfural, Hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF), Acetic Acid, Formic Acid and the sugars Cellobiose, Glucose, Xylose and Arabinose. For the quantification of the compounds, it was necessary to use analytical curves. Due to the different chemical properties of the analytes of interest, curves with different intervals were obtained for each of the analytes. For acids, the range of 0.05–1 g L−1 was used with detection at 210 nm; for Furfural, the range of 1–40 mg L−1 and for 5-HMF, 0.1–10 mg L−1, both at 274 nm. Sugars were determined by refractive index with a range of 0.5–10 g L−1.

The supernatant was evaluated by UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy, after showing luminescence on exposure to 350 nm lamp radiation. The absorption spectra in the UV/Vis region were obtained using an UV–Vis-NIR Cary 5000 Series, Agilent Technologies spectrophotometer. The spectra were collected in a wavelength range of 800–200 nm, using a quartz cuvette of 1,0 cm path length. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The samples were excited at different wavelengths between 230 and 490 nm, and submitted to spectrophotometric scanning up to 800 nm, with excitation and emission windows of 5 and 10 nm, respectively, using a quartz cuvette with a 1,0 cm optical path.

In this work, considering what is proposed in scheme 1, we evaluated the materials obtained from the solid fraction (carbon-based solid acid catalyst) and the liquid fraction, (the supernatant obtained), divided into 3.1 Catalyst Production and characterization, and 3.2. Investigation of the liquid fraction of the carbonization/sulfonation process.
MCC catalysts were prepared under heating conditions at three different temperatures (100 °C, 125 °C and 150 °C). Fig. 1 shows the appearance of the sulfonated catalyst produced at 125 °C.

Materials that achieve carbonization have low water solubility and are readily precipitated with the addition of water in the reaction medium, as can be seen in Fig. 1. According to Tao et al. (2015) carbon-based acid catalysts can be obtained from various raw materials, present strong acidic groups and hydrophobic properties that are responsible for the low solubility of these catalysts in water despite the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonic groups in the case of sulfonated materials.

The MCC carbonization/sulfonation at 100 °C, regardless of the H2SO4:MCC ratio and reaction time, was not efficient in producing uniform carbonized materials, leading to no precipitation of the final product. The appropriate experimental conditions to produce sulfonated carbonaceous material in the simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation process depend on the type of lignocellulosic source, it is observed that the increase in temperature beyond the range considered ideal leads to a decrease in the mass yield and sulfonation degree of the materials, the same fact is observed for lower temperatures (Koskin et al., 2020; Lou et al., 2008; Malins et al., 2016). Zailan et al. (2021) observed that for most polysaccharides and amorphous carbon materials, the ideal sulfonation temperature is observed to be between 100 and 150 °C, depending on the substrate.
The effect of temperature during the simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation reaction over the carbon-based material yield and ion exchange capacity are presented in Table 1 .

The results observed in Table 1 shows that the highest mass yields and ion exchange capacity (IEC) are found at a temperature of 125 °C and the highest H2SO4:MCC ratio (20,1, v:w). Considering that sulfonation occurs in graphitic structures, the carbonization process must initially occur for the sulfonation to be successful. Thus, the results show that in the presence of sulfuric acid, at temperatures of 125 °C and 150 °C, MCC presents satisfactory carbonization, which favors sulfonation. The increase in the ion exchange capacity shows that the carbonized samples have hydrophilic groups linked to their structure, among them the sulfonic groups. Although the reactions made with H2SO4:MCC ratio of 10:1 (v:w) were successful in obtaining a solid catalyst, the reaction mixture for these preparations underwent solidification during the heating step, contrary to the reactions carried with reagents ratio of 20:1, which made these mixtures more difficult to handle in the rinsing step and the acid removal more laborious. Thus, the H2SO4:MCC ratio of 20:1 (v:w) was chosen to produce the SACs to be further characterized.

To evaluate the functional groups, present in the obtained products, the carbonized/sulfonated samples were characterized by infrared absorption spectroscopy (FITR) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) - Dispersive Energy Scattering (EDS) and the results were presented in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). From now on, the catalysts samples will be named according to the reaction conditions of its production as SAC x/y, being x the reaction temperature, y the reaction time.
Fig. 2 shows the infrared absorption spectra, recorded from 1900 to 400 cm−1, for SAC125/1 and SAC150/1 samples, produced at 125 °C and at 150 °C, respectively.

The SAC, produced in this work, is a complex material that has a graphitic structure and carboxylic and sulfonic functional groups, as can be seen in the infrared absorption spectra in Fig. 2. In the infrared spectra, bands were identified that are associated with the formation of a carbon-based material with graphitic structure and bands associated with oxidation and sulfonation confirmed by the presence of carboxyl and sulfonic groups, respectively. The two catalysts present: a band at 1690 cm−1, assigned to the stretching of the CO bond, a band at 1565 cm−1, attributed to the CC stretching of the aromatic rings, which is confirmed by the presence of the low intensity peak at 869 cm−1 associated to = C-H out-of-plane deformation of the aromatic rings; these bands indicate the formation of a graphitic structure and confirm the carbonization. In addition, the presence of the band at 1690 cm−1, shows the presence of carboxylic groups that partially contribute to the ion exchange capacity values observed (Adolfsson et al., 2015). In addition to these bands, SAC presents bands attributed to the presence of sulfonic groups, these bands appear at 1150 cm−1, assigned to O=S=O stretching of the sulfonic groups and 1025 cm−1 assigned to SO stretching. These results show the formation of hydrophilic groups on the surface of the samples and confirm the sulfonation of the SACs (Lou et al., 2012).
The catalysts produced were characterized by SEM and EDS in order to evaluate the morphology of the SACs and confirm the presence of sulfur, the results are shown in Fig. 2(b).

The SEM micrographs of the catalyst produced at 125 °C has a structure in the form of rigid plates with no apparent porosity. The development of the graphitic structure in the presence of sulfuric acid occurs simultaneously with partial oxidation and sulfonation reactions, such processes can interfere with the formation of porous structures due to the action of the strong sulfonate agent on the structure of the material being formed. Ngaosuwan et al. (2016) observed the same behavior for other carbon-based acid catalysts produced by sulfonation. The SAC 150/1 sample has larger particles when compared to SAC 125/1, possibly due to the aggregation of the more hydrophobic material; this sample also does not have an apparent porosity like SAC 125/1.

The analysis of the composition of the samples, carried out by EDS, confirms the presence of sulfur atoms in the SAC 125/1 and SAC 150/1 samples, with a higher sulfur percentage for SAC 125/1, as can be seen in Fig. 3 . In addition to the bands observed in the FTIR spectra, the EDS results confirm the sulfonation of the material and this data, together with the ion exchange capacity results, show a significant sulfonation of the SAC.

As the samples produced at 125 °C showed the best results regarding carbonization/sulfonation mass yields, ion exchange capacity and sulfur content, this samples were selected for further tests after a new evaluation of the mass yield and ion exchange capacity as a function of reaction time for 6 replicates. The results are summarized in Table 2 .

Table 2 presents the percentage mass yield after the simultaneous carbonization/sulfonation reaction and the IEC of the samples produced between 1 and 6 h. It is observed that there are no significant differences in the mass yield obtained as a function of reaction time and the IEC values are slightly higher for shorter reaction times. In this sense, considering the mass yield and IEC results, the SAC is efficiently produced in 1 h of reaction. However, all samples produced at 125 °C were characterized in terms of chemical and structural properties and in relation to catalytic activity in esterification reactions.

The morphology of SACs 125 was characterized by SEM, as shown in Fig. 3(a). All samples have irregular rigid particles that do not show apparent porosity. It is still important to note that the particles observed are similar from one sample to another, indicating that there is practically no influence of temperature on the formation of carbon-based particles in the temperature range studied.

X-ray diffraction analysis provided similar results for all analyzed samples, as can be seen in Fig. 3(b). In the x-ray pattern, three peaks are observed: a more prominent one centered at 2θ = 24°, referring to the 002 plane, associated with graphitic plane sheets oriented in a considerably random fashion; a less intense one at 2θ = 41°, associated with the plane 100 of polycyclic aromatic carbon structures; and another, at 2θ = 12°, referring to the 004 plane of graphite sheets with oxidized faces (Mintz et al., 2021).
The Raman spectra analyses complemented the structural evaluation of the sulfonated carbon-based materials produced and are shown in Fig. 3(c).

The Raman spectrum of SACs 125 (1 to 6 h) show two bands at about 1367 cm−1 (D band) and 1593 cm−1 (G band). The D band is related to disorder in the carbonaceous structure, resulting from sp3 carbon bonds, structural defects, or sp2 domain disorder; G band otherwise is assigned to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms, associated to crystallinity of carbon in the graphitic structures arranged in planar configurations (Ferrari, 2007).

The ID/IG ratio can be used as an estimative to the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG ratio obtained for the samples was around 0.71. Graphite has an ordered structure that has only a few active Raman bands in the spectrum, with a weak intensity D band of disorder and a more intense G band that is attributed to the vibration of the crystalline lattice, which translates to low values of ID/IG (Mohan et al., 2016). Therefore, low ID/IG values indicates the existence of a slightly deformed graphitic structure with a low degree of defects. The increase in this ratio may also indicate an increase in the amount of surface oxides, their presence being expected considering the raw material used.

Catalyst stability was evaluated by Thermogravimetry Analysis (TGA), TGA/DTG curves are shown in Fig. 3(d). As observed for other characterization tests, the catalysts presented the same profile for its thermogravimetric decomposition curves with practically the same thermal stability. The observed TGA profile is also similar to that of sulfonated materials presented elsewhere in the literature (da Luz Corrêa et al., 2020; Farabi et al., 2019).

In the TGA curve shown in Fig. 3 (d) can be observed 5 thermal events associated with the decomposition profile of the catalyst in an inert atmosphere: stage 1 (40–175 °C) referring to the evaporation of adsorbed water or generated by reactions of material dehydration, responsible for a mass loss of ~10 %; the second stage (175–450 °C) has as main information the decomposition of the sulfonic groups on the surface of the catalyst, followed by other processes observed in stage 3 (450–615 °C), referring to the decomposition of the oxygenated groups COOH and CO at the surface of the solid, contributing a mass loss of ~20 %; in stage 4 (615–760 °C) there is a mass loss of just over 5 %, probably referring to the decomposition of oxidized sp2 structures in the graphitic core; finally, at 760–950 °C, there is a gradual mass loss, attributed to the unoxidized fractions of the sp2 structures (Mintz et al., 2019).

To evaluate the catalytic activity, oleic acid was used as a molecule to be modified in a reaction with methanol catalyzed by the SACs prepared at 125 °C. Each catalyst was tested in three consecutive reaction cycles, then regenerated with concentrated H2SO4 and used in one more esterification cycle. The reactions were carried in three replicates and the results are shown in Table 3 and Fig. 4 .

The results from the catalysis tests shows that all fresh catalysts presented similar activity, yielding an average of 78 %–81 % conversion in the first use. Except from the SAC 125/1, all catalysts showed a loss in activity upon reuse, resulting in smaller yields for each consecutive cycle, while presenting similar results between them. On the other hand, the SAC 125/1 showed an increase in catalysis yield for the second use, although it decreases for the third use but still presents a superior yield than the other catalysts for the same number of cycles. After regeneration with concentrated sulfuric acid, all materials recovered its initial activity, considering the standard deviation of the results.

The main mechanism behind the deactivation of carbon-based sulfonic SACs are leaching of acid sites, carried mostly by methanol used in the reaction, the poisoning by water generated as a byproduct, and the conversion of the acid sites into carboxylic and sulfonate esters on catalyst surface. The regeneration with concentrated sulfuric acid is efficient in the catalyst regeneration for its ability of replenishing leached sulfonic sites and hydrolyze surface esters, restoring these deactivated sites (Fraile et al., 2015, 2012; Roldán et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the liquid fraction of the carbonization/sulfonation process
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatant fractions were investigated to evaluate the presence of other substances with potential application in the production of biofuels and/or materials that can be efficiently used in energy generation. Therefore, the supernatant was evaluated by different techniques: i) High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) to verify the presence of fermentable sugars and other cellulose acid degradation products, and ii) Irradiation with UV radiation at 350 nm for research of species that show photoluminescence.
Table 4 presents the species analyzed by HPLC and the byproducts quantified in the supernatant.

In the analysis of the supernatant by HPLC, 5-HMF (Hydroxymethylfurfural), Furfural, formic acid and acetic acid were found for SAC 125/1. Fermentable sugars were not detected for any sample in the experimental conditions adopted according to the results presented in Table 4. For the SAC 125/6 supernatant, only acetic and formic acids were detected.

From these data it can be assumed that, within the first hour of reaction, all saccharides produced by cellulose acid hydrolysis are readily converted into furfural and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural, which are gradually converted through the dehydration steps in acidic medium to formation of carbonized material, so that they are no longer identified after 6 h of reaction, being observed for this sample (SAC 125/6) only fractions of acetic acid and formic acid, products of hydrolysis and degradation of saccharides. The production of the catalyst at 125 °C for 1 h, allows the efficient production of the carbon-based SAC and allows to preserve some substances in the supernatant such as 5-HMF. 5-HMF is a byproduct from the carbonization/sulfonation reaction of microcrystalline cellulose that has great appeal in green chemistry processes since, due to its chemical structure, it is an important molecule to produce furan-based products for many applications in several industry sectors, as packaging, textile, cosmetics, food, and health.

In the investigation of the SAC preparation liquid fraction (supernatant), the solutions optical properties were evaluated, since carbon dots (CD) can be obtained in the liquid fractions of production of carbon-based solids by pyrolysis, carbonization and acid treatments assisted by microwaves (Adolfsson et al., 2015; Ahuja et al., 2022). For this exploration, two liquid fractions of the SACs produced at 125 °C for 1 h and 6 h were separated, being the supernatants of the first and the last filtration. These solutions were named after the respective SACs from which they were obtained, as CD 125/1 or CD 125/6.

The liquid fraction resulting from the SAC preparation showed a bright bluish green color when irradiated with UV radiation at 350 nm. The absorption spectra in the UV–Visible region are shown in Fig. 5 .

Fig. 5 shows the normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra for the solutions CD 125/1 and CD 125/6. The pH of the solution resulting from the first filtration is strongly acidic (pH = 0) while the rinsing solutions at the end of the filtration process have a pH close to 5.0. Two effects contribute to the different profiles observed in the UV/Vis absorption spectra: the influence of the reaction time for SAC production and the pH of the supernatant. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the sample shows high absorption with the spectrum extending up to 600 nm; it is possible to observe shoulders and bands between 210 and 260 nm that are generally attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds in aromatic polycyclic structures. Between 280 and 360 nm, it can be seen a shoulder attributed to the n-π* transitions of oxygenated functional groups as CO and CO (Mintz et al., 2021; Nguyen et al., 2019).

The normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra in the region between 280 and 370 nm show an increase in intensity for the CD solutions at pH 5, different from that observed for the CD solutions at pH 0 which presents lower intensity in this region with little extension for longer wavelength values. This effect is even more evident for the samples CD 125/1, for which a red shift is observed for the samples at pH 5. For solutions CD 125/6, the behavior observed is quite different: the bathochromic shift is not observed and the increase in intensity for the sample at pH 5 is lower than that observed for the sample with the same pH of CD 125/1 solution.

It is important to point out that more than an increase in absorption intensity the UV/vis spectrum for the sample at pH = 5.0, there is a change in the spectrum profile of the bands become broader and shows an shifts towards red with increasing pH. The existence of functional groups on the carbon dot surface, as well as the presence of other species in the aqueous solution, significantly influence the interactions between nanoparticles and nanoparticles and the species in the aqueous medium. It is important to consider that at pH = 0, there is a large amount of sulfuric acid that interacts with the carbon dots preventing their aggregation and therefore we observe a profile of narrower peaks and little extended for high wavelength values. This environment is drastically modified at pH 5. The existence of functional groups on the carbon dot surface, as well as the presence of other species in the aqueous solution, significantly influences the interactions between CDs and CDs and the species in the aqueous medium. It is important to consider that at pH = 0, there is a large amount of sulfuric acid that interacts with the carbon dots preventing their aggregation and therefore we observe a profile of narrower peaks and little extended for high wavelength values. This environment is drastically modified at pH 5. The increase in intensity and the shift of the absorption band may be associated with the process of protonation/deprotonation of the carboxyl group. The effect of pH on the optical properties of CDs is still a matter of great debate, however we can cite as main mechanisms the electronic transition processes and the change in the colloidal stability of the medium that can generate aggregation of nanoparticles in higher pH medium (Ahuja et al., 2022).
The photoluminescence spectrum of CD 125/1 aqueous solutions with different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 6(a).

Fig. 6(a) shows the photoluminescence (PL) profiles of the CDs solutions generated for different excitation wavelengths (λexc). It is generally observed that the PL spectra shows a bimodal curve when excited with radiation wavelengths between 270 and 330 nm which can be attributed to the presence of different emitting functional groups. Although the same behavior is observed for both solutions, for CDs at pH = 5.0 the bimodal profile of the PL curve is less accentuated, suggesting that the pH of the medium, thus the surface groups protonation, plays an important role in the luminescence mechanism. As the emission wavelength increases, a red shift is observed. Fig. 6(b) shows the emission wavelength shift profile as a function of excitation wavelength for aqueous solutions of CD125/1 at pH = 0 and pH = 5.

There is a tendency for the emission peak to red shift, an aspect observed in the photoluminescence curves, which can be seen in detail in Fig. 6(b). In Fig. 6(b) i) although a red shift trend is observed as a function of the excitation wavelength, the results for the CD 125/1 pH = 0 presents some randomness and are not uniform, which may indicate different emitting species considering the presence of carbon core and hydrophilic functional groups on CD surface. For pH = 5, 6(b) ii), the effect of the red shift is evident, with less randomness, due to the reduction of the bimodal profile, which may indicate less superficial heterogeneity. The differences observed are related to the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and other functional groups on the surface of CDs that are sensitive to the pH of the medium. According to Syamantak et al. (2018), deprotonated and protonated species have different emission wavelengths and the interconversion between them results in a relative population of two emissive species.

It is worth to stress that there are morphological differences between the CDs produced under different conditions, which can also influence the optical behavior of the solutions. The explanation for the observed behavior is still controversial and needs further investigation, an aspect that will be addressed in future works (Kong et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2021; Syamantak et al., 2018).

The CD solutions were stored under refrigeration (~8 °C), without contact with light and reanalyzed in a UV–vis spectrophotometer and a fluorimeter two months after production. The samples showed no changes in the absorbance and fluorescence profiles, indicating stability of these nanoparticles in solution. Due to the observed characteristics of good stability and λexc-dependent PL, the carbon dots obtained can be considered promising nanomaterials for application as chemical sensors, bioimaging and other biomedical applications (Boruah et al., 2020; Chai et al., 2019). Properties such as the size of CDs and their functionalization can influence the photocatalytic properties of these systems and expand their application in energy conversion processes, water splitting, hydrogen evolution, water treatment, among others (Sakdaronnarong et al., 2020).

In this work, simultaneous celulose carbonization and sulfonation was efficient for the production of a sulfonated solid acid catalyst (SAC) and Carbon Dots (CD). SAC catalyzes the oleic acid esterification reaction converting more than 80 % to methyl oleate, with activity about 3 cycles and potential to be regenerated. In the supernatant, hydroxymethylfurfural was detected by HPLC and CD were characterized by spectroscopic techniques. The simultaneous production of SAC and CD is a viable low-cost process, as it does not involve high pressure and temperature treatments, with the advantage of carbonizing and sulfonating the cellulose in a single step.

M.C.G. Souza: Conceptualization, Investigation, methodology, software, and analysis; A.C.F. Batista: Investigation, methodology; R. F. Cuevas: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation W. J. F. da Silva Filho: Investigation, methodology; M. A. G. Balanta: Data curation, Formal analysis; Ana M.C. Farfan: Data curation, Formal analysis; Validation R. M. N. de Assunção: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank to Finaciadora de Estudos e Projetos (Grant Numbers 01.11.0135.00 and 01.13.0371.00) and the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior for scholarship.
